And Salt Lake had a newspaper article that if Boise was connected to Salt Lake and Salt Lake was reconnected to Vegas, which is what they're wanting. They were saying it would take you from Boise through to Salt Lake to Vegas 17 hours. I don't know how much this is going to cost. And I if, if, if I take the train, I want to see the scenery. Yeah. My luck is they're going to go through the most scenic part of that trip, which is through Rainbow Canyon down in Nevada at night. That's just my luck, you know. Yeah. So again, pitching this to a 20 year old, a 30 year old, you know, a 50 year old. I can drive to Vegas in less than six hours. I can fly in an hour. Why would I take a train for 17 hours? Yeah. Yeah, I that's the thing. We look back on the railroads with nostalgia of the streamliners, the Liners, the Portland Rose and City of Los Angeles and beautiful cars. And everyone's sitting in the lounge car eating these beautiful meals. And that's not what Amtrak is anymore. And they they it's different. And back then that was the fastest mode of transportation. Yep. Even in the 40s I have a 1940s car and it tops out at 55. You can go maybe 100 miles on a tank of gas if you're lucky. Um, yeah. Get on the rails in four days or less, in three and a half, three nights. You know, you can be across the country in the world of all right in the world revolved around around that transportation method. Uh, but now you need to be in New York in four hours. You can't be taking the train. You know, it's just like if we invented teleportation on Star Trek and we asked the same person, you can beam yourself to New York, or you can get on an airplane and take there in four hours. Why would I take a break at that point? Yeah. So yeah, it'd be another mode of transportation for Idaho. It's definitely a scenic route. It's definitely something to to, to take. I mean, I was thinking about taking the train through Canada. It's a scenic train. And and it's that's your that's the trip. I'm, I'm, that's my vacation is I'm getting on in Vancouver and I'm going to fly home from Toronto. That is my vacation. It's a week on the train, but it's planned that way. It's not because I need to get across the country to go to Disney World, you know? You know, having a commuter train locally that's been talked about too. Everyone wants one. Oh yeah, the freeway. And let's use the line we have. Well, there's a lot of upgrading. I just talked to the organization that was doing the surveys and everyone wants a train, but we're going to need to have another track built next to the current freight track on the Boise branch line. Can't use that rail then to get between Nampa to Caldwell. Union Pacific owns all of that right of way. There's no more room for any more rail, and UP already told the local company here. The people doing the surveys don't block my industry, because if you put a third track in, you're going to cut right through a siding. That access is an industry. So that's going to be tough. Um, and they're estimating it to be over 6 Billion to build a commuter rail from Caldwell to Boise. Oh boy. So the I so when asked when will this happen they says I don't know ten years. Well 50 years, 100 years and maybe never because of the the money. Where's it going to come from. It's the new commuter system. Even the Los Angeles commuter system today, which is they call it, they're rebuilding the old whole Pacific Electric, that what the old interurbans were was it was a for profit business. So was the Boise and Urban. You buy a ticket, it pays for the upgrading. It pays for the everything. Yeah. People stop buying tickets. There's no more profit. There's no more business. A lot of these new things are tax dollar. They're subsidiary. You know the base that you you buy a ticket. Yeah that does help. But it's not going to go belly up because it is being bankrolled through taxes and other things like that. So. Right. Yeah I don't know uh what eventually it's going to get so bad that, yeah, traffic's going to get so congested that people are going to want a commuter system, an alternative. Yeah. By that time, it's too late because we bought all the land up. We built everything on everywhere. We need a private I always thought would be good was to run Boise branch line, get to Nampa, take the commuter rail, fly up over the freeway, come down the center median and use the median all the way to Caldwell. Well guess what, they've widened. The freeway median is gone. Okay. So your your your land where these right of ways can go are going are disappearing. The old interurban is all a highway. State highway 2020. No that's up there. It's the state street all the way to Middleton and Eagle. That was the and then. Yeah right. Highway 44, Caldwell Boulevard. Ustick. These are all big four lane roads now. Those are all part one. Time was the two lane dirt road and a interurban was on the edge of it. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Well we'll see. Uh, I'm certainly at the age where I don't expect anything within my lifetime, but, uh, one last thought that I had. You can comment on this as you like, is I know you have a hobby that involves old train rails. Tell me about how you can get out on an abandoned line and ride with abandon. Yeah, so I have like a what's a go kart on rails, you could say. It's it's a square frame with four train wheels and a little Honda engine on the back, and you put it on the rails and you can ride the rails. And up in North Idaho, we still have some of the old rail lines that are no longer in use. There's only really one section that is officially abandoned. Most of these rail lines are owned by a railroad company, so anytime you're on the tracks, you are trespassing. It is illegal unless you have permission from the railroad company. Um, I do have permission when I ride here locally in Emmet on the Idaho Northern line up through up through Horseshoe Bend area. I do get a, I get a what's called a track warrant, which is a permit to be on the property and ride between milepost 39 to milepost 50, and that's where I can ride. Otherwise, through research, Google Maps, and driving around the whole state of Idaho for a long time. You kind of learn what's around and you know, the rails are rusted or grown over. It's still owned by somebody, but there's no train service on them anymore. So a little safer to ride. And so I've been able to go up the north Idaho, and I have a, a wink and a nod to, um, kind of, you know, keep a low profile, and I and I ride up there, poke around. Yeah, I've taken it down to Nevada and I've gotten permission from the Nevada Northern Railway, which is an excursion line slash historic museum now. And I volunteered, volunteered down there, and I've been able to ride their rails in the evening. Oh. And, um. So it's becoming a hobby. Some people are enjoying it. They have pedal power rail bikes, and these clubs are starting all over the nation. And they're actually being these rail bikes are being bought by excursion companies. So when they're not running their excursion train, they're renting out the bikes and people get to pedal down their track. And yeah. And it's just a another way to see the you see things you don't get to see on the rails. Yeah. It's a different view isn't it. So that was a hobby started many years ago and through, um, through driving around North Idaho and seeing these tracks, all rusty rails heading into the forest. The idea was like, you know, that would be really cool to be able to go explore that area and more than just walking down the tracks. And then I saw a PBS outdoor item. It was an Outdoor Idaho episode on public television, and they did a story on railroads in Idaho. And at that time, the Camas Prairie Line was still in service all the way up to Cottonwood through Craigmont. And they actually I remember the video, they actually had a Speeder Club that got permission to ride that whole railroad all the way up through there. And so they were Idaho Public television's documenting it, and they actually got to talk to, um, a man named Dick Smart, who was a man in Coeur d'Alene that invented he took what was it was Sears and Roebuck and the catalog. You could buy an apparatus, an outrigger, that would attach to a bicycle, and it was called a rail bike. And you could use it to access the rails and ride the rail, and you could buy it in the catalog. So he took that design and he kind of improved on it. And he built his own outrigger and he rode the. What is now the trail of the Coeur d'Alenes bike trail back then, in the 80s. It was still a railroad track that was no longer used. So he wrote a whole book. And started this new thing called rail biking. And other people joined in and built their own rigs and, um, but PBS highlighted all that. So I took that show and told dad, hey, we should build that. And in the end, what dad goes, it's easier just to put four wheels on the rail and build a cart and we'll just, you know, have a bench seat. And that's how our rail bike, which was originally pedal power, got started. Oh, all through PBS show. And my idea of exploring the rails, I was like, this is we should do that. And then. Yeah, trial and error. Yeah, yeah. Well, I want to thank you again. Eric. This has been a very delightful discussion. Entertaining, educational, informative and, uh, there may be some other topics that we want to hook up again on and make some separate sort of recordings on. Okay. But, uh, we know each other and where we, uh, live, so we'll stay in touch. I'm sure That'll be great. Yeah, I've got one's not railroad. Well, not railroad, but through Owyhee County history, we I've just written three separate articles that are being put into the Owyhee Outpost publication, a book that we print. And one is on, um, who was James Guffey? The man who's the bridge is named after the railroad bridge and Swan Falls Dam. History. The electrifying the Swan Falls Dam, the very first on the snake, the very first AC power in Idaho. And it was privately owned by the Trade Dollar Mine Company out of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. Oh, wow. So this whole history of the very first, um, high voltage alternating current in Idaho started at Swan Falls. And the Dewey substation, which is still standing, is the very first AC sub in Idaho. So there's a whole little interesting history of how these Pittsburgh mining, uh, Pittsburgh rich, very rich men like James Guffey. He was the founder of Gulf Oil. Really? Yeah. And he brought oil rigs out to Owyhee County to drill for oil. Didn't find any, but, uh, oil well Gulch out in Wyoming County. Uh, Wayne County was named that because they had all these oil rigs out there trying to trying to find oil and. Yeah, but that's a whole I've done a lot of recent this past year and the year before, um, digging into that history. Yeah, we could do a whole little history on it. We'll stay in touch then. Okay, well. Thanks then. You bet. Have a good week. Alrighty. Bye.

lines.line: # Introduction and Project Overview

> The interviewer introduces himself and the oral history project, outlining its connection to the America 250 commemoration and the Idaho Historical Society. The goals of the project are explained, emphasizing the importance of community engagement and the recognition of Idaho's role in America's legacy. The interview's purpose is set: to explore Idaho's railroad history and its impact on settlement and prosperity.

metadata.title: Introduction and Project Overview
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer introduces himself and the oral history project, outlining its connection to the America 250 commemoration and the Idaho Historical Society. The goals of the project are explained, emphasizing the importance of community engagement and the recognition of Idaho's role in America's legacy. The interview's purpose is set: to explore Idaho's railroad history and its impact on settlement and prosperity.

lines.line: # Permission and Name Confirmation

> The interviewer requests permission to record the interview and asks the interviewee to state and spell his name for the record. This establishes the formal start of the interview and ensures proper documentation of the participant's identity.

metadata.title: Permission and Name Confirmation
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer requests permission to record the interview and asks the interviewee to state and spell his name for the record. This establishes the formal start of the interview and ensures proper documentation of the participant's identity.

lines.line: # Personal Background and Education

> The interviewee shares his educational journey, beginning with high school and continuing through college, where he pursued a degree in history. He describes his early recognition as a history student, his transition to Boise State, and his involvement with train museums, including restoration projects and tour guiding. His professional trajectory leads to his current role as director of the Owyhee County Museum.

metadata.title: Personal Background and Education
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee shares his educational journey, beginning with high school and continuing through college, where he pursued a degree in history. He describes his early recognition as a history student, his transition to Boise State, and his involvement with train museums, including restoration projects and tour guiding. His professional trajectory leads to his current role as director of the Owyhee County Museum.

lines.line: # Early Experiences and Memories with Trains

> The speaker recounts childhood experiences with trains, including family vacations, museum visits, and riding the Grand Canyon Railway. He describes his father's involvement with steam locomotives and his own early fascination with trains, which was fostered by living near a railroad crossing and collecting train toys. These formative experiences led to a lifelong passion for railroads and history.

metadata.title: Early Experiences and Memories with Trains
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts childhood experiences with trains, including family vacations, museum visits, and riding the Grand Canyon Railway. He describes his father's involvement with steam locomotives and his own early fascination with trains, which was fostered by living near a railroad crossing and collecting train toys. These formative experiences led to a lifelong passion for railroads and history.

lines.line: # Union Pacific and Competing Railroads in Idaho

> The discussion turns to the major railroad companies that crossed Idaho, with a focus on Union Pacific, Northern Pacific, and Great Northern. The interviewee explains the timeline of their arrival, their spheres of influence, and the nature of competition, particularly in northern Idaho. He notes instances of joint ownership and rivalry, highlighting the strategic importance of these railroads in the region.

metadata.title: Union Pacific and Competing Railroads in Idaho
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to the major railroad companies that crossed Idaho, with a focus on Union Pacific, Northern Pacific, and Great Northern. The interviewee explains the timeline of their arrival, their spheres of influence, and the nature of competition, particularly in northern Idaho. He notes instances of joint ownership and rivalry, highlighting the strategic importance of these railroads in the region.

lines.line: # Notable Idaho Railroad Projects and Branch Lines

> The interviewee shares his interest in unique Idaho railroad projects, such as the Camas Prairie line with its famous trestles. He discusses local history, including the Boise, Nampa and Owyhee Railroad, and other lesser-known lines like the San Francisco, Idaho and Montana Railway. The conversation covers ambitious but incomplete projects, the transformation of rail lines into trolley routes, and the repurposing of railroad grades for highways, illustrating the diversity and complexity of Idaho's rail history.

metadata.title: Notable Idaho Railroad Projects and Branch Lines
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee shares his interest in unique Idaho railroad projects, such as the Camas Prairie line with its famous trestles. He discusses local history, including the Boise, Nampa and Owyhee Railroad, and other lesser-known lines like the San Francisco, Idaho and Montana Railway. The conversation covers ambitious but incomplete projects, the transformation of rail lines into trolley routes, and the repurposing of railroad grades for highways, illustrating the diversity and complexity of Idaho's rail history.

lines.line: # Boise Depot History and Community Impact

> The focus shifts to the construction of the Boise Depot in the 1920s, exploring why the city invested in a new passenger depot despite having an existing facility downtown. The interviewee explains the community's desire for direct transcontinental service and the negotiations with Union Pacific. The depot's architectural significance and its role in shaping Boise's identity and urban development are discussed, highlighting its enduring value as a landmark.

metadata.title: Boise Depot History and Community Impact
metadata.synopsis: The focus shifts to the construction of the Boise Depot in the 1920s, exploring why the city invested in a new passenger depot despite having an existing facility downtown. The interviewee explains the community's desire for direct transcontinental service and the negotiations with Union Pacific. The depot's architectural significance and its role in shaping Boise's identity and urban development are discussed, highlighting its enduring value as a landmark.

lines.line: # Morrison-Knudsen Restoration and Depot Preservation

> The interviewee reflects on the restoration of the Boise Depot by Morrison-Knudsen, noting that he was not present during the project but appreciates its impact. He describes the improvements made, such as updating infrastructure and restoring original features, and emphasizes the importance of the restoration in preserving the depot for future generations. The challenges of balancing historical accuracy with modern accessibility are acknowledged.

metadata.title: Morrison-Knudsen Restoration and Depot Preservation
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee reflects on the restoration of the Boise Depot by Morrison-Knudsen, noting that he was not present during the project but appreciates its impact. He describes the improvements made, such as updating infrastructure and restoring original features, and emphasizes the importance of the restoration in preserving the depot for future generations. The challenges of balancing historical accuracy with modern accessibility are acknowledged.

lines.line: # Unanswered Historical Questions and Railroad Construction Labor

> The conversation addresses gaps in Idaho's railroad history, particularly regarding the labor force that built the railroads. The interviewee notes that while some information exists about Irish, Chinese, and Mormon workers, many personal stories and details have been lost to time. He suggests that further research could uncover more about the contractors and workers involved, highlighting the need for deeper exploration of these overlooked aspects.

metadata.title: Unanswered Historical Questions and Railroad Construction Labor
metadata.synopsis: The conversation addresses gaps in Idaho's railroad history, particularly regarding the labor force that built the railroads. The interviewee notes that while some information exists about Irish, Chinese, and Mormon workers, many personal stories and details have been lost to time. He suggests that further research could uncover more about the contractors and workers involved, highlighting the need for deeper exploration of these overlooked aspects.

lines.line: # Railroad Expansion, Settlement, and Economic Impact

> The discussion explores the rapid expansion of railroads in Idaho, driven by economic motivations and the desire for efficient routes. The interviewee explains how railroad construction prioritized terminal points and business interests, leading to quick settlement and development along the lines. He provides examples of opportunistic branch construction to access resources, illustrating how railroads shaped Idaho's growth and economy.

metadata.title: Railroad Expansion, Settlement, and Economic Impact
metadata.synopsis: The discussion explores the rapid expansion of railroads in Idaho, driven by economic motivations and the desire for efficient routes. The interviewee explains how railroad construction prioritized terminal points and business interests, leading to quick settlement and development along the lines. He provides examples of opportunistic branch construction to access resources, illustrating how railroads shaped Idaho's growth and economy.

lines.line: # Interurban and Commuter Rail Development in the Treasure Valley

> The interviewee describes the evolution of local transportation in the Boise area, focusing on the development of interurban electric railways. He explains how trolleys expanded to connect communities throughout the valley, eventually fading with the rise of automobiles and improved roads. The role of Nampa as a hub for multiple branch lines is discussed, highlighting the changing landscape of commuter and freight rail service.

metadata.title: Interurban and Commuter Rail Development in the Treasure Valley
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee describes the evolution of local transportation in the Boise area, focusing on the development of interurban electric railways. He explains how trolleys expanded to connect communities throughout the valley, eventually fading with the rise of automobiles and improved roads. The role of Nampa as a hub for multiple branch lines is discussed, highlighting the changing landscape of commuter and freight rail service.

lines.line: # Interurban Connections and Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show

> The interviewee explains how interurban lines connected with mainline railroads, allowing for the transfer of freight and special events. He shares a story about Buffalo Bill Cody's Wild West show arriving in Boise via the interurban tracks, illustrating the flexibility and integration of local and national rail systems. These connections enabled unique experiences and contributed to the region's cultural history.

metadata.title: Interurban Connections and Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee explains how interurban lines connected with mainline railroads, allowing for the transfer of freight and special events. He shares a story about Buffalo Bill Cody's Wild West show arriving in Boise via the interurban tracks, illustrating the flexibility and integration of local and national rail systems. These connections enabled unique experiences and contributed to the region's cultural history.

lines.line: # Prospects for Train Travel Returning to Boise

> The interviewer asks about the possibility of restoring train service to Boise, including Amtrak and commuter rail options. The interviewee discusses the challenges of profitability, infrastructure, and competition with other transportation modes. He reflects on the nostalgia of train travel, the practical limitations of modern rail service, and the financial and logistical hurdles facing any potential revival, both for long-distance and local commuter trains.

metadata.title: Prospects for Train Travel Returning to Boise
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks about the possibility of restoring train service to Boise, including Amtrak and commuter rail options. The interviewee discusses the challenges of profitability, infrastructure, and competition with other transportation modes. He reflects on the nostalgia of train travel, the practical limitations of modern rail service, and the financial and logistical hurdles facing any potential revival, both for long-distance and local commuter trains.

lines.line: # Railbiking Hobby and Exploring Abandoned Rail Lines

> The interviewee describes his personal hobby of railbiking, using a custom-built rail cart to explore abandoned and unused rail lines in Idaho and other states. He explains the legal and logistical aspects of riding on these tracks, the process of obtaining permission, and the appeal of discovering hidden parts of the landscape. The hobby is connected to a broader movement of railbike clubs and excursions, offering a unique way to experience railroad history.

metadata.title: Railbiking Hobby and Exploring Abandoned Rail Lines
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee describes his personal hobby of railbiking, using a custom-built rail cart to explore abandoned and unused rail lines in Idaho and other states. He explains the legal and logistical aspects of riding on these tracks, the process of obtaining permission, and the appeal of discovering hidden parts of the landscape. The hobby is connected to a broader movement of railbike clubs and excursions, offering a unique way to experience railroad history.

lines.line: # Closing Remarks and Other Historical Interests

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude and a promise to stay in touch for future discussions. The interviewee mentions his ongoing historical research, including articles on James Guffey, the Guffey railroad bridge, and the electrification of Swan Falls Dam. He shares intriguing details about early AC power in Idaho and the involvement of Pittsburgh financiers, suggesting further topics for exploration beyond railroads.

story.title: Eriks Garsvo: Idaho Train history
story.description: The interview focuses on Idaho's train history, featuring insights from Eric Scalzo, the director of the Hawaii County Museum. It explores key developments in Idaho's railway system, touching on the role of railroads like Union Pacific in shaping Idaho's growth. Scalzo discusses specific lines such as the Camas Prairie Line and the Boise depot project, highlighting their historical and economic impacts. The conversation also covers past and potential future train services in Idaho, and Scalzo's personal experiences and projects, including rail biking and historical research on Idaho's railway construction and significant figures like James Guffey.
